item.title: POV you're taking your cosplay gf out for dinner - YuzuPyon
item.description: Hi everyone hope you like my handmade Irulan chainmail dress cosplay ? I'll be posting a full tutorial video on how I made it on my Youtube Channel if you're interested in seeing the process feel free to follow my account to not miss future videos : https://youtube.com/@yuzupyon Pics of my Irulan cosplay in the desert : https://www.instagram.com/p/C59BBbnPf-4/ Have a great day everyone! -YuzuPyon
item.dc:creator: yuzupyon

item.title: Redhead Monday
item.description:
She’s adorable!!
Siryn my beloved
Stefon voice: this dump has everything! Kissing boys! Horses! Sexy maids! Poison Ivy! Crowley! Horns! Xmen! Witches! Lizards! Dogs?! Cats! Wieners!
Tantomile tax- y’all know I pay my taxes like billionaires oughta but especially on a short dump like this so I apologize now for all the pets
Coricopat tax
Liza Jane tax
Froggy tax
Scruffy tax
Nessie tax

item.title: Monday Eve Dump
item.description:
So punny!
I can hear his voice
I hate how true this feels
*existential dread
One opportunity
I still try an olive every year to see if I like them yet
*me after the Superbowl
This is correct
Just thought this was super cool
Ha!
Cat tax. Have a good night everyone!

item.title: Women appreciation edition 2
item.description:
Amy Irving and Carrie Fisher IN NEWSBOY HATS
Amy Irving
Jane Goodall- I love this picture so much
Rosario Dawson
Eva Noblezada
Rita Moreno!!
Rita
Chita Rivera
Ashley Park
Jasmine Cephas Jones
this is so neat!! Great Gatsby is on Broadway right now with Jeremy Jordan and Eva Noblezada (my two favorite singers) and I would do anything to go see it
Ann Margret (she was in Newsies!!)
WHAT A BADASS
Ann Reinking
Ann
I LOVE HER NOSE ITS LIKE MINE!!
Ice Spice for my boyfriend because he loves her. He wants to dress up as her for Halloween (he has ridiculously curly hair) which I am so excited about because it would probably involve him in a crop top.
Bryce Dallas Howard
Nessie tax

item.dc:creator: wolfwinter

item.title: Amateur Dad Built-in Closet
item.description:
It had been 12 months since I’d finished my last closet project. Long enough to recover (physically and mostly mentally) but not nearly long enough to forget how consuming it would be. Nevertheless, here is the process of my amateur attempt at converting our wire shelf storage into a built-in closet
Another before and after from a different angle
It all starts with a plan, which was mostly formulated from googling “built-in closets.” I used online modeling software Sketchup to play with the design until I got it where I liked it (and got it co-signed by my wife). You may notice the design has some doors where the final version does not, but more on that later
After finally coming up with a design I liked, it was time to gut the room. Wire shelving, base molding, and carpet all had to come out to reveal the blank canvas upon which this monstrosity would be built.
I purchased this Dewalt laser level (DW0822CG) which was super helpful for the layout. Highly recommend if you were considering something similar. The accuracy more than made up for the cost, plus I’m sure it will be useful in future projects
After getting the layout scribed on the floor, ceiling, and walls, I built the base out of 2x4s. I also included some underlayment under the 2x4’s so the future base molding would fit the future vinyl plank
After laying down 3/4 maple ply over the 2x4’s (the corners were more difficult to cut than I’d like to admit), I installed 2 pieces of preassembled furniture. My amatuerness has not evolved enough where I’m confident doing anything with hinges or slides. The piece on the left is an IKEA Tarva dresser with the legs removed. The piece on the right was purchased with doors to act as a linen closet. The sheets along the back wall are the doors for the closet that are not in use in the finished product. Installing these 2 pieces first gave me a sturdy base for all the other pieces. These were secured into the base and studs behind the walls.
3/4 maple plywood was used for almost of the closet structure. Above I’m adding two plywood sheets on either side of the linen closet to divide the sections from one another
Since I would be wiring the closet with 24v LED lighting, I needed a place to run the wires. I routed out a 5/8” x 3/8” deep channel in certain dividers so I could fit a wire holder which would eventually house the low voltage wires. I used the factory edge of a plywood scrap as a straight edge to ensure the channel was (mostly) straight. Along with the clamps on either end, I shot a couple 18 gauge nails in the straight edge to ensure it stayed put
Progress! Adding additional dividers and ceiling pieces. I also installed a tarp on the left side to keep dust out of the other section of the closet. I picked up the tarp and the zipper thing at Lowe’s. It was very effective at limiting the dust to just this room
Here’s another shot with almost all the structure in place. 3/4 plywood on the backs may have been overkill but it really makes the whole thing feel solid. It was also helpful on the far wall as our son’s is on the other side and it cut the noise down. I cut the back pieces at certain height to line up with future shelves so you wouldn’t be able to see the seams
The section on the right was particularly tricky because I opted to use a post here instead of another wall divider on the end. I thought it would make it feel more open (it does) but it was a bit more difficult to secure than I thought. I had the post custom built from 1” maple sheets at a cabinet shop. I used pockets screws to secure the bottom
The top didn’t line up with a ceiling joist so I had to install something to fix it to. The pic above is in our attic. After brushing away the insulation, I installed the 3 horizontal 2x4’s to fix the post and the ceiling plywood on top of this section. After carefully measuring , I screwed several screws from the top of the far 2x4 into the post. The post only split once so I considered it a win. This part was kind of a fun mini project that I had never done before
After getting the structure competed, it was time for shelves! I used pocket screws to fix them to the dividers and post, respectively.
The back left corner drove me absolutely nuts for 2 reasons. A. I had to cut these in an L shape and each shelf was a different size (slightly). And 2. The middle divider(where you see the green laser) was NOT plum when I installed it. The orange level you see is acting as a brace to push the divider towards plum. It ended up working surprisingly well, but it was an unexpected challenge.
My buddy Shay helping me install some shelves
1x2’s were used as the faces of the shelves and dividers. Each of these was glued and installed with 18 gauge nails. Much patching compound was consumed for the nail holes (but less than was consumed for the pocket screw holes). I used MH ready patch and drydex.
After getting the 1x2’s up, I took a stab at crown molding for the first time. It was difficult but not as difficult as I was led to believe. I purchased a Kreg Crown Pro jig which made it infinitely easier to cut. Despite all the guides, i had to throughly think about each cut before I made it.
Here the crown is all completed and getting ready for paint. 2 crucial steps that weren’t captured. A. I used MH Ready Patch between the top of the 1x2’s and each shelf and sanded until flush. This was very helpful in getting a nice finish. And 2. Caulking every corner and seam on this took way longer than I care to admit. I was so happy to be done
As much as I thought the caulking sucked, painting was my least favorite part of the project. The closet got 1 coat of primer and 2 coats of Sherwin Williams Emerald urethane. I applied it with a cabinet roller I got from Lowe’s. I was really going to spring for a paint gun (or ask to borrow one) but I didn’t want to risk learning on all the tight spaces
After getting the paint done, I happily moved onto the electrical. All the lights are 24v LEDs. Here are the parts: EMITEVER COB LED Strip Lights using RGB LED Light Strip Connectors with Maxbrite 18 AWG wire in cut-to-fit hunhun 3.3ft/1Meter V Shape LED Aluminum fixtures. These really elevated the project. I didn’t include them in the “linen closet” on the right as this was originally supposed to be behind doors.
Following lighting, it was time to move onto flooring. I used vinyl plank that was already in place on the other side of the closet. If you’re ever wondering in you can install LVP, you can do it! This was very satisfying and relatively easy compared to the rest of the project - at least for me
I added a threshold for the transition from the bathroom tile to the LVP. Here is it upside down so you can see the liquid nail application
After the floor was down, I could add the base molding
On top of the base molding, I added quarter round for the transition into the bottom shelf. I was particularly happy with the miters on this corner. Again, I used MH Ready Patch between the seams to make it look like one pieces. These were also glued and nailed together
After painting the base molding, I drilled some new holes for the dresser drawers. I borrowed the hole guide for these, but it was relatively straightforward. I did have to sand the sides of some drawers to make them fit. It’s MOSTLY plum but I did have to “make it work” more than once
And here’s another completed pic. The doors on the right side where we were going to have a linen closet did not fit like I thought they would. I attribute this to: A. The whole thing not being completely plum (however at least it looks plum/level)! And 2. I may have set the 1x2’s a little close to the edges so there wasn’t enough room when they opened. I gave it the college try when installing them but was doing more harm than good.
Another picture that includes the other section of the closet I completed last year. Link for that if you’re interested: https://imgur.com/gallery/DgUUwIR
If you’ve made it this far, thanks for reading! Here’s another pic of my helper, Shay.
item.dc:creator: SturgicalStrike
